It is not called business. This is investing. What you consider when it comes to investing is the relationship between risk and return. A well-diversified investment portfolio reduces your exposure to risk. Money held in Piggy Flex (not Safe lock, where you are paid upfront) can be accessible at any time. While you don't have a need for the money, it earns interest until you need it. Also, Safelock is not a good option for me. Note, this is my POV, I want to have access to my cash when I need it. So, never Safelock any money you would in a short time. In business, cash is King, and the short turnover period is an indicator of profitability. Again, leaving your money in PiggyVest is not business. It is savings, which earns interest.

500,000 Naira minus 4 Naira is 499,996 Naira. Saving 500,000 Naira in Nigeria is tough due to low incomes and high living costs. We're now pretty far off the original discussion of income, but here's some data: Mobile: * Nigeria's usage gap (# connected but not using mobile internet) has been stagnant for the last 5 years. About 60% of those with coverage do not use mobile internet. (GSMA) * Overall smartphone affordability in rural areas has fallen off a cliff. Handsets now costs 99% of monthly income for poorest 20% vs. 55% a couple years ago (GMSA) * Smartphones sales overall in Nigeria have been lagging rest of Africa (1% vs 3% YoY). Higher end brands like Samsung fell 30%. (Tecno) Food Insecurity: * There's been almost a doubling of those who are food insecure in Nigeria since 2023 (UN) * 40%+ food inflation meaning much less of wallet can go towards other things (NBS) Exports: * Both oil and natural gas exports have fallen on a volumes basis over time. * Oil is at a lower barrels per day than its historical average and about 60% of its production 10 years ago. * Natural gas exports has been steadily dropping since 2021 in terms of cubic meters. Savings / Income ('23 and '24 Piggyvest Surveys): * Those saving every month dropped 17% YoY. Those with an emergency funds dropped 4% * Earners above 500k naira dropped 8% YoY. No income responses increased 8%.

Safety-Related Pros and Cons of Laminated Side Windows Recently, many new vehicles have been adopting laminated glass for side windows. Laminated glass consists of two layers of glass with a plastic layer (PVB) sandwiched between them. While it has long been standard for windshields, its use in side windows is increasing to enhance crash safety. Below are the safety-related advantages and disadvantages. (Note: This information is based on general automotive industry standards and research, and may vary by specific vehicle model.) Advantages Crash Protection and Debris Prevention: Laminated glass doesn’t shatter into pieces upon impact, maintaining its shape and reducing the risk of passengers being injured by glass shards or ejected from the vehicle. This is particularly effective in rollover accidents, helping to keep body parts inside and increasing survival rates. UV Protection and Added Safety: The plastic layer blocks up to 99% of UV rays, reducing skin damage and interior heat buildup, which indirectly contributes to safety by reducing driver fatigue. Noise Reduction for Improved Focus: It reduces wind and road noise, enhancing driver concentration and indirectly helping prevent accidents. Enhanced Security: Laminated glass is harder to break, making it more resistant to theft or break-in attempts, improving the safety of parked vehicles. Disadvantages Difficulty in Emergency Escape: Laminated glass is highly durable and resistant to breaking, even with standard escape tools (e.g., window-breaking hammers). In emergencies like submersion or fire, this can make escape nearly impossible, increasing survival risks. This has become a significant concern with recent vehicles. Increased Weight Affecting Handling: Laminated glass is about 25% heavier than tempered glass, increasing the vehicle’s overall weight. This can reduce fuel efficiency and slightly affect stability during high-speed driving or sudden braking due to changes in the center of gravity. Overall, laminated side windows significantly improve crash safety but pose challenges for emergency escapes. Manufacturers are working on solutions like specialized escape tools. When purchasing a vehicle, it’s recommended to check the specific model’s specifications and consult with experts if needed.

The #Pokémon Trading Card Game Pocket app has replaced the #HoOh and Lugia EX card illustrations from its new "Wisdom of Sea and Sky" set after an internal review confirmed that incorrect reference materials had been provided to the illustrator, leading to plagiarism accusations from artist #lanjiujiu. The original immersive art, which resembled lanjiujiu's 2021 fan art, was temporarily removed on July 30 and replaced with a "New Art Coming Soon" message before the updated designs were added. The #Johto-themed set was released the same day. Despite some controversy, the mobile app, created by The #Pokemon Company, Creatures Inc., and DeNA, has generated over $100 million since its launch in October 2024.

1/5 — Early Experiments and Skepticism Early attempts to make Pokémon cards into NFTs were unofficial and raised IP concerns. In February 2024, the Pokémon Company clarified that it “has not developed or approved” any NFTs related to its brand and warned that any NFTs using Pokémon intellectual property are “unofficial and in no way affiliated”. Fans had speculated that the new Pokémon Trading Card Game Pocket app might integrate NFTs, but the developer insisted it would focus on digital card collecting without blockchain. This early phase was marked by rumours, unauthorised projects, and skepticism from traditional Pokémon fans.

If you are a freelancer or entrepreneur, this particular hack has helped me save money so easily. 1. Save anything between 10-20% from any gigs you are paid for immediately the money lands your account and before you start to spend it. 2. Save 5-7k on a daily basis. With platforms like Piggyvest this can be so seamless. 3. The 10-20% from your gigs, lock it and let it grow so you don’t tempt yourself to think you have any money (you have to practically pretend that money doesn’t currently exist). For me, I lock it up for a year to get maximum interest (for instance, 20% interest if I lock money up for 1 year, meaning 200k becomes 240k) 4. Rinse and repeat this and make it a hobby. 5. Check how much you have saved in 3 months. Many of you who think you aren’t even millionaires will start to believe.

Start Small With Investing One big mistake people make is waiting until they have plenty of money before they start investing. Truth is, investing is less about the amount and more about the habit. Even ₦10k / $10 a month into mutual funds, savings bonds, or an ETF builds consistency. That habit trains your mind to think long-term and creates discipline around money. 👉🏽 The earlier you start, the more time your money has to grow. Compound interest is slow at first, but years down the line, it works like magic. Don’t fall for the lie that only the rich invest. The rich became rich because they started where they were. Pick a safe beginner option — e.g. Cowrywise, PiggyVest (Nigeria) or Vanguard ETFs (US/UK). Automate your monthly contribution, even if it’s small. Here's a beginner's path to build toward deploying capital like in the post, leveraging your grit and learning speed. Start small, scale up. 1. **Educate yourself**: Take free online courses on Coursera/YouTube (e.g., entrepreneurship, finance basics). Read books like "Rich Dad Poor Dad" or Nigerian blogs on business. Focus on simple models like freelancing or reselling. 2. **Gain experience via side hustles**: Freelance on Upwork/Fiverr (writing, marketing) or local gigs (tutoring, delivery). Aim to earn ₦50k-100k/month initially. Use apps like PiggyVest/Cowrywise to save 20-30% automatically. 3. **Source capital**: Save from hustles; ask family/friends for small loans (₦100k start). Apply for grants (e.g., Tony Elumelu Foundation) or crowdfunding on GoFundMe. Avoid high-interest loans early. 4. **Test a model**: Start with low-capital ideas like dropshipping or service biz (e.g., social media management). Invest small (₦50k) in tested opportunities, track returns. 5. **Scale**: Reinvest profits, network on LinkedIn/X for mentors. Aim for 10-20% monthly returns initially through compounding. Stay consistent; it takes 6-12 months to build momentum. Research risks thoroughly.

I realized today that most of us want to make money, but most people won’t really get it because they have no reference point. So they don’t really understand fully what it is they want. Of course I’d explain - Wealth means different things to different people; and these meaning is usually influenced by our past & present experiences - so what we want is somewhat in between. If your parents never built a house, and you build a house - they would say you have achieved a whole lot Yes you have (building a house is not easy in this economy): but based off their reference point they’d wonder why you still work hard and what else you want out of life. This is the point where they say - “relax, you have achieved more than most people” This is caused by reference points; you on the other hand have seen richer people - so you tell them: “no mummy, I still need to push” You feel my drift? The higher your reference point the bigger your appetite, the bigger your appetite the big the opportunity you’d chase This is why you go on to make 10x what your parents made. Now here is where the problem is - your reference point too is small when compared to another person’s reference point. This morning I got a terrible hit to my reference point after I went through Temi Otedola’s tiktok: @temiotedola I watched her life, compared it to where I am now and felt completely far behind. There is a life so full of ease and luxury it is absolutely mind blowing. And I don’t have it now, and I want it. …and I saw how different my life is when compared with what she has. Then I imagined what her reference point would be and shuddered. Mr Eazi is a hard working guy, great guy - but he left music and became part owner of a billion dollar business after he started hanging with the Otedola kids. My guess is his reference point changed. Reference point controls the kind of business you do, the level of wealth you chase and ultimately the level of wealth you get. I just realized that a few hours ago, and it has sent cold shivers down my spine. I was recently watching a series called: “House of Usher” - It is a horror story about a billionaire. When any of his kids comes of age they would come to pitch a business to him and if he likes it he funds it. His last son comes to him and pitches a night club: his idea is grand but the man waves it off. Then said (I will paraphrase) - “an Usher builds something that changes the world, not a business that marks up Glenfiddich” - It is a movie but it just hit me; most of the times the reference point we have is so small all we do is buy and sell. Then others with a bigger point of reference build Paystack…Flutterwave…MoniePoint…Piggyvest…Andela. It is insane. A big real estate tycoon once advised me to move to Lekki Phase 1. He said that was what he did that made him rich. Waking up and seeing rich people… …and seeing them do rich people things; it somehow stops you from playing too small. Now I fully get it; it increases your point of reference. If you stay around people where the biggest achievement is a nice car and a nice house that would be all you would ever want. Point of reference is something else and boy! Teni Otedola’s tiktok is a whole lot of point of reference - in one video she did a walk through of her father’s house and it made it clear to me what I want when I fantasize about being rich. When you have not been near real wealth before, you know you want it but somehow you still don’t know what it is you want. Until you see it on a tiktok account like I did today and you know - clearly - what it is you want, and how frigging beautiful it is.

NTB stands for Nigerian Treasury Bills—short-term government securities issued by the Central Bank of Nigeria to raise funds, with maturities of 91, 182, or 364 days. They're low-risk and offer fixed returns. To find a financial advisor in Nigeria: - Contact banks like UBA or FirstBank for in-house services. - Use apps like PiggyVest or Cowrywise, which offer guidance. - Search platforms like Upwork for freelancers, or firms like FSDH Asset Management. - Look for certified pros via SEC Nigeria's website or local directories. Always verify credentials and consider fees.

To start investing in Nigerian Treasury Bills (NTBs): 1. Use apps like PiggyVest or Cowrywise: Download, sign up, verify ID, fund your wallet, and select NTB options. Minimum: ₦100–₦1,000. 2. Or via banks (e.g., UBA, FirstBank): Open an account, apply for NTBs. Minimum often ₦100,000+. Latest rates (Sep 3, 2025 auction): 91-day yield ~15.94%, 182-day ~16.81%, 364-day ~21.49%. Returns depend on tenor and amount; they're discounted upfront. Consult a financial advisor for risks.

Today, I just randomly remembered the time I was strongly advised against getting a car because it would make me ‘less desirable to men’. So, I had finally reached my target savings to get a car after a year of setting up my piggyvest account. Then, I recalled that a mommy in church had given me a lift one time and she recently got the car too. I don’t know much about cars but I really loved how hers looked on the inside and outside. Luckily for me, I was privy to the cost of the car purchase and it worked perfectly with my budget. Added to this, the mommy had mentioned in passing that she had a trusted mechanic who had accompanied her for the car purchase. In light of all these, she seemed like the perfect person to ask for car purchase advice and directions. You know, to link me up with her dealer, and then maybe let her mechanic tag along with me for a test drive and everything. Oh, how wrong I was! That was how, with Jesus joy in my heart and 6M in my virtual wallet, I cornered mommy after church service to seek counsel. Men and brethren, counsel, I got! But from a very different approach. Church mommy sat me down, and she began preaching to me. “Why do you want a car?” “Who would marry a single woman who is already driving around town without caution?” And by driving around town, she meant me driving myself to work and church. Also, I didn’t realise that single women driving around town was reckless behaviour. “How will men see you? You want to drive men away?” She continued. I sat through her sermon, while I silently chided myself for coming to her. Na me mess up. I don’t know what I was thinking or how I forgot this was the same woman who, if we were discussing and I mistakenly let it slip that I used a washing machine for my clothes or that I give my clothes to the laundry man, would hush me, advising me not to wash my dirty linen outside. That day, I listened to her attentively, thanked her for her ‘motherly’ advice, then proceeded to buy the car. The following Sunday, I made sure to park right beside hers. The shock on her face gave me pure satisfaction. It’s been two years and this mommy still believes that the car is the reason for my ‘singleness’. What mommy has failed to understand is that I do not desire to attract a man who is intimidated by something as ordinary as a car. A second-hand car for that matter. Mtchew. Sadly, I have come to realise that some of our older mommies in church don’t truly understand the concept of compatibility, especially in the area of logic and core values. Now, why would I want a man, who is intimidated by my career growth, by my success? Why should I choose mediocrity to keep a man? If having a car is the reason a man doesn’t pursue me, then he is clearly not my type. And honestly, it’s good riddance! Mind you, I'm not one to toot my own horn. However, for the context of the story, I have to add that I am not a materialistic person. I have never been driven by material possessions. I have never been moved by what a man has or does not have. And even though I do have a car, I don't mind if he doesn't have a car. What really matters to me are three non-negotiable things. One, he must be a lover of God, a genuine lover of God. Secondly, he must have something upstairs. He must be someone who thinks, someone who can hold conversations, someone who has a mind that can process thoughts, yes? And thirdly, he must be self-driven, who doesn't need to be motivated, someone who will push me as well to my dreams, someone who has a dream burning in his heart. It has never mattered if he has nothing, because the above, in my opinion, is everything. It’s been two years, and I am still single but no, I don’t believe it’s because I have a car or am driving recklessly around town. #Fiction

What To Do As A Beginner Before Entering A New Month If you’re starting from zero, don’t chase big money. Chase consistency. Set a baseline. Not $1,000. Not $3,000. Start with $200. Your only goal: Hit that $200 before the month ends. Some people will say it’s small. That’s fine. But let’s be real… According to PiggyVest Savings Report, a large number of Nigerians earn less than ₦100k monthly. So if you can generate consistent income online, you’re already ahead. Now here’s how to actually do it: Show up daily and apply to jobs daily Send proposals Post your work Offer simple services (automation setups, CRM fixes, landing pages) No overthinking. Just execution. Now the part most people get wrong: They win once… and switch strategy. Don’t. Repeat it. If you can make $200 for 2–3 months consistently, you’ve built leverage. Now scaling becomes simple: $200 → double effort → $400 $400 → improve your offer → $500+ $500 → build systems (GoHighLevel, n8n, automations) → $700 $700 → better positioning → $1,000+ You don’t scale what you haven’t mastered. Disclaimer: I’m not saying you can’t make $1,000 in your first month. You can. But can you repeat it? Because one good month is luck. Consistency is a system. This new month, forget hype. Forget screenshots. Focus on predictable income. Because once money becomes predictable, scaling becomes inevitable... Remember it's One workflow at a time...

PiggyVest vs Cowrywise. I have written about this before but this time I have checked their features and will like to settle this once and for all, PiggyVest was built for people who need their money working harder than they do. With Investify returning up to 35% per annum across real estate, agriculture, and commercial papers, and SafeLock paying your interest upfront before the lock period even ends, PiggyVest is unapologetically about maximising every naira. Add HouseMoney for rent, dollar savings, and automated deductions that won't let you cheat yourself, and you have a platform designed to make excuses impossible. Cowrywise, on the other hand, is for people who think beyond the next quarter. It sits on top of 20+ mutual funds from Nigeria's heaviest fund managers like Stanbic IBTC, ARM and United Capital, and actually builds you a portfolio. It hedges your wealth against the naira with dollar investments, runs a full Halal savings mode for users who refuse to compromise their faith for profit, and forces you to stay the course with strict no-early-withdrawal rules. That is not a bug. That is the entire point. PiggyVest wins the short game. Cowrywise wins the long game. The people arguing online about which is better are the same people who will still be broke in five years because they spent time debating instead of doing both. PiggyVest for the grind. Cowrywise for the vision.

What are your chances of creating something really big that a lot of people would adopt? The chances are higher if nobody else has ever done it, and you don't get discouraged while doing it. The most important part of it all is making sure that it is not something others can also easily do. Never let them see you coming. I still remember trying to raise money for a super agent network in Nigeria because the banks were ripping us off. I rightly assumed that the best way to fight the banks was to build a bank, but I wrongly assumed that it would be called a bank. The African neo-bank phase fizzled out quickly as all the ambitions didn't amount to anything. The interesting part of it is that in a place like Nigeria, the true neobanks were the payments, savings, and lending companies that became massive. Opay, Moniepoint, Piggyvest, Fairmoney. My initial theory was that it was best to go narrow and deep before spreading out. We wanted to get merchants as agents and build upon them. Then the banks saw us coming as we depended on them. What is interesting was that they also saw Interswitch and Etransact coming as well. They also fought telcos to a standstill for 20 years!! Those that truly became bigger than the banks never tried to fight the banks. They just served the customers quietly and quickly until they became bigger than the banks. Opay was doing motorcycle rides and Moniepoint was just a back-end processor when they were TeamApt. Fairmoney is still a microfinance bank. Piggyvest is just an app. I am writing a book on building African Banks and this part of the story is a fascinating one. There are also other entities that will soon become bigger than we think and they are also in plain sight already. What will become your competition doesn't always have to look like you but will always serve your customers at their point of need. Never let the incumbents see you coming.

One thing I just confirmed about life? That phase will always pass lol Prepare for the best, prepare for the worst You can be at your 100+ today make “unforeseen circumstance” come carry you go minus lol Your savings fit no save you especially when you lock am for PiggyVest like me lol Your business capital fit no dey at reach (or liquid) Your accrued income go just dey accumulate but not liquid enough to drop on your laps Nobody would come to save you Except God And the gift of men he’s given to you So now, it’s in your hands to protect and preserve relationships Do all you can to build a valuable relationship I’m always “all by myself” lol But I’m jovial so, “Many people knows me” But not “many people knows me” I know many people, but I don’t talk to “many people” I’ve always been so uptight about life, I give to my friends and random people who ask me for help but I can’t just find myself to be vulnerable enough to ask even when it’s a matter of life and death lol. This year, I said I’m gon let go of that emotional ego (I learnt, that’s the reason) So I’m learning how to be vulnerable How to ask without feeling terrible about myself Ps. I literally feel like, I’d die if I open my mouth to ask lol I’m always careful about the aftermath lol But then again, How careful can we be for a life that is so unpredictable? And then, for how long will I keep playing “the saviour that need not to be saved” So I’d rather die? lol Go for days without eating till God sends “a miraculous help”? lol I thank God, For the gift of men he’s given to me I thank God For the friends that see through my laughter, my jokes, my memes, hyper activeness on social media and my nonchalance to certain things, I thank God I am the last person in this world to “open up” fully lol I won’t I cannot I just can’t 😂 Cos sometimes it would be hooking me in my throat😭 So I stay silent, Yes I’d rather die in the pain than spill Cos in my head, What if they don’t really care lol What if they don’t even care enough to help lol What if they don’t even have the capacity to help lol What if I’m going to be a burden to them lol “Just stay still and silent you’d find a way to sort it out like you’ve always done lol” That has always been the case, but I said no more in 2026 I feel like God has given me people who are exactly like me Like my tribe of like minds but I can’t see them cos I’ve not opened my eyes and minds to see them I always want to “solo help” myself to avoid “see finish” But these people don’t even have that kind of horrible mind lol They’re just like me😂 Waiting for someone to open up and ask Look for ways to help and lift the person out of being vulnerable Then “Get up on your feet and keep waking” kind of support Hence, I’m grateful to God and I’m grateful to men🙏🏻 This was a really hard decision for me to make in the early days of this year, but I’ve decided that it’s the best way to grow. Because, how do we grow in depth and not just surface, if nobody can be vulnerable? Sighs This life, Thank you Jesus and thank you my friends for loving me🥹🙏🏻❤️ Thank you Jesus for the gift of men I’m grateful🥹🙏🏻❤️

You've been seeing everybody talk about the Piggyvest saving report and 150k average salary. You've not had time to read the report because is too long for you? Here is the summary of everything: 1. Nearly 3 in 5 (almost 60%) Nigerians earn below ₦100,000 monthly or have no income at all. 2. The number of Nigerians saving consistently has dropped from 64% in 2023 to just 40% in 2025. 3. Food and groceries now consume the vast majority of income (72%) 4. Saving to leave the country ("Japa") fell from a top 3 goal in 2023 to the 8th position in 2024. 5. Over 3 in 5 income earners support extended family members. Middle children pay the most "black tax" than firstborns. 6. Despite the reputation for tech earnings, 40% of adult Gen Z reported having no income last year, and 69% lack emergency savings. in fewer words, things are getting worse.
A lot of people dismissing the Piggy Vest report did not actually read the entire thing. They just saw the headlines and ran off with it.
